---
title: "Why MTTR Alone Misleads"
subtitle: "Add MOVA to Measure Real Risk"
author: "Caleb Kinney"
date: "BSides Charm 2026"
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import pandas as pd
import polars as pl
from datetime import datetime
from great_tables import GT
from plotnine import (
    aes,
    element_blank,
    element_line,
    element_rect,
    element_text,
    facet_wrap,
    geom_line,
    ggplot,
    labs,
    scale_color_manual,
    scale_x_continuous,
    theme,
    theme_minimal,
)

metrics = pl.read_parquet("data/metrics.parquet")
summary = pl.read_parquet("data/summary.parquet")

STRATEGY_LABELS = {
    "oldest_first": "Oldest-First",
    "newest_first": "Newest-First",
}
METRIC_COLORS = {"MTTR": "#355C7D", "MOVA": "#C05621"}
BACKLOG_COLOR = "#8B1E3F"

plot_metrics = (
    metrics
    .with_columns(pl.col("strategy").replace(STRATEGY_LABELS))
    .select(
        "strategy",
        "month_index",
        pl.col("mttr_days").alias("MTTR"),
        pl.col("mova_days").alias("MOVA"),
    )
    .unpivot(
        index=["strategy", "month_index"],
        on=["MTTR", "MOVA"],
        variable_name="metric",
        value_name="days",
    )
    .to_dicts()
)
plot_metrics = pd.DataFrame(plot_metrics)

backlog_plot = (
    metrics
    .with_columns(pl.col("strategy").replace(STRATEGY_LABELS))
    .select("strategy", "month_index", "aged_over_180")
    .to_dicts()
)
backlog_plot = pd.DataFrame(backlog_plot)

summary_table = (
    summary
    .with_columns(
        pl.col("strategy").replace(STRATEGY_LABELS),
        pl.col("mttr_days").round(1),
        pl.col("mova_days").round(1),
    )
    .rename({
        "strategy": "Strategy",
        "mttr_days": "MTTR (days)",
        "mova_days": "MOVA (days)",
        "open_count": "Open",
        "aged_over_180": "180+ days",
    })
    .to_dicts()
)
summary_table = pd.DataFrame(summary_table)

shared_theme = (
    theme_minimal(base_size=18)
    + theme(
        figure_size=(10.2, 4.2),
        legend_position="top",
        legend_title=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_minor=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_x=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_line(color="#d8d2c7", size=0.4),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill="#fffdfa", color=None),
        plot_background=element_rect(fill="#fffdfa", color=None),
        strip_background=element_rect(fill="#ebe5da", color="#ebe5da"),
        strip_text=element_text(weight="bold"),
        axis_title=element_text(color="#374151"),
        axis_text=element_text(color="#374151"),
        plot_title=element_text(weight="bold", color="#111827"),
        plot_margin=0.1,
    )
)

ojs_define(
    metrics_js=(
        metrics
        .with_columns(
            pl.col("strategy").replace(STRATEGY_LABELS),
            pl.col("month").dt.strftime("%Y-%m"),
        )
        .select(["strategy", "month_index", "month", "mttr_days", "mova_days"])
        .to_dicts()
    )
)
```

## A Quick Question {.center}

::: {.metric-big .centered}
What would you do
if your MTTR doubled next month?
:::

::: notes
Open with the question and pause. Let them answer it in their head before you give them the twist.
:::

---

## Most dashboards imply one answer

- Something slowed down
- The team is getting worse
- Leadership should worry

::: notes
That reaction is common because MTTR is usually presented as a score, not as one view into system behavior.
:::

---

## But that reaction can be wrong {.center}

::: {.metric-big .centered}
MTTR can go up
while risk goes down
:::

::: notes
Say this once, cleanly, and do not over-explain yet.
:::

---

## The broader issue

::: {.lede}
MTTR isn’t the problem, it’s an example of a broader issue: metrics that measure activity instead of exposure.
:::

::: notes
This frames the talk as systems thinking, not metric evangelism.
:::

# Why This Matters

## Metrics shape behavior

If the metric defines success, teams will optimize the metric.

---

## That is fine when the metric matches the outcome

It is dangerous when it does not.

---

## Security teams feel this constantly

- dashboards reward closure speed
- reports compress nuance into one number
- incentives drift toward optics

::: notes
This is the leadership slide. Keep it grounded in operating reality.
:::

---

## MTTR is a useful example

Not because it is bad.

Because it is incomplete.

# What MTTR Actually Measures

## MTTR is a flow metric

Time to remediate **closed** vulnerabilities.

---

## It tells you something real

How quickly completed work moves through the system.

---

## It does not tell you

- what is still open
- how old the open backlog is
- whether exposure is shrinking right now

---

## One line version {.center}

::: {.metric-mid .centered}
MTTR measures completed work,
not current exposure.
:::

::: notes
This is the definition the audience should remember.
:::

# Add MOVA

## MOVA is simple

**Mean Open Vulnerability Age**

How old is the risk we still carry?

---

## Think stock and flow {.tight}

| Metric | Question |
|---|---|
| MTTR | How fast are we closing work? |
| MOVA | How old is what remains open? |

---

## You need both

One tells you about throughput.

One tells you about exposure.

---

## One caution

Means can hide ugly tails.

---

## Example

95 vulnerabilities are 7 days old.

5 vulnerabilities are 400 days old.

The mean looks manageable.
The tail does not.

::: notes
Use this to justify the aged-backlog chart later.
:::

---

## So report the tail too

- MOVA
- open count
- 180+ day backlog

# Real Implementation

## The data model is small

```{python}
import polars as pl
from datetime import datetime

vulns = pl.DataFrame({
    "id": [101, 102, 103],
    "created_at": [
        datetime(2026, 1, 10),
        datetime(2026, 2, 5),
        datetime(2026, 3, 1),
    ],
    "resolved_at": [
        datetime(2026, 2, 1),
        None,
        None,
    ],
})
```

::: notes
Three columns matter for the metric logic: identifier, creation date, resolution date.
:::

---

## Same table, first question {auto-animate="true"}

```{python}
mttr = (
    vulns
    .filter(pl.col("resolved_at").is_not_null())
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("resolved_at") - pl.col("created_at"))
        .dt.total_days()
        .alias("days_to_fix")
    )
    .select(pl.col("days_to_fix").mean().alias("mttr_days"))
)
```

::: notes
Closed only. Duration to closure. Mean. That is MTTR.
:::

---

## Same table, second question {auto-animate="true"}

```{python}
as_of = datetime(2026, 4, 1)

mova = (
    vulns
    .filter(pl.col("resolved_at").is_null())
    .with_columns(
        (pl.lit(as_of) - pl.col("created_at"))
        .dt.total_days()
        .alias("open_age_days")
    )
    .select(pl.col("open_age_days").mean().alias("mova_days"))
)
```

::: notes
Open only. Age as of today. Same schema, different filter, different truth.
:::

---

## The implementation point {.center}

::: {.metric-mid .centered}
This is not a new platform.
It is a different lens.
:::

# A Simple Simulation

## The question

Can MTTR go up while risk goes down?

---

## Setup

- 24 months
- 360-item starting backlog
- 60 new vulnerabilities each month
- 60 fixes each month
- same system, same capacity

---

## The only thing that changes

How the team prioritizes the next 40 fixes.

---

## Two strategies {.tight}

| Strategy | What gets fixed first |
|---|---|
| Oldest-First | longest-open findings |
| Newest-First | most recently discovered findings |

---

## The core loop

```{python}
def simulate(strategy: str) -> pl.DataFrame:
    for as_of in months:
        current = active_vulns(backlog, as_of)
        to_fix = select_to_fix(current, 60, strategy)
        backlog = resolve_vulns(backlog, to_fix["id"], as_of)
        rows.append({
            "mttr_days": compute_mttr(backlog, as_of),
            "mova_days": compute_mova(backlog, as_of),
        })
```

::: notes
This is intentionally small. Same arrivals, same capacity, same data model. Only the ordering changes.
:::

---

## Why this works as a demo

It isolates the incentive.

Nothing else moves.

# Results

## Same workload, different signal

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-alt: "Faceted line chart comparing MTTR and MOVA over 24 months for oldest-first versus newest-first prioritization."
(
    ggplot(plot_metrics, aes(x="month_index", y="days", color="metric"))
    + geom_line(size=1.5)
    + facet_wrap("~strategy", ncol=2)
    + scale_color_manual(values=METRIC_COLORS)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=[1, 6, 12, 18, 24])
    + labs(
        title="Higher MTTR can still mean lower exposure",
        x="Month",
        y="Days",
    )
    + shared_theme
)
```

::: notes
Read the left panel first. Then the right. The contrast is the story.
:::

---

## Oldest-First {.center}

::: {.metric-mid .centered}
Higher MTTR.

Lower MOVA.
:::

It looks slower while the open backlog gets younger.

---

## Newest-First {.center}

::: {.metric-mid .centered}
Lower MTTR.

Higher MOVA.
:::

It looks faster while older exposure stays behind.

---

## The takeaway from the chart {.center}

::: {.metric-big .centered}
MTTR can go up
while risk goes down
:::

::: notes
Make the comparison explicit: switching from newest-first to oldest-first worsens MTTR and improves exposure.
:::

# Show The Tail

## Aged backlog tells one story

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-alt: "Faceted line chart showing the number of open vulnerabilities older than 180 days for oldest-first and newest-first prioritization."
(
    ggplot(backlog_plot, aes(x="month_index", y="aged_over_180"))
    + geom_line(color=BACKLOG_COLOR, size=1.5)
    + facet_wrap("~strategy", ncol=2)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=[1, 6, 12, 18, 24])
    + labs(
        title="Newest-first leaves a larger 180+ day tail",
        x="Month",
        y="Open vulnerabilities",
    )
    + shared_theme
)
```

::: notes
This is the backlog MTTR never shows. It makes the exposure problem visible.
:::

---

## End state comparison

```{python}
#| echo: false
(
    GT(summary_table)
    .tab_header(title="After 24 months")
    .cols_align(align="center")
    .fmt_number(columns=["MTTR (days)", "MOVA (days)"], decimals=1)
)
```

::: notes
The point of the table is not beauty. It is side-by-side comparison once the chart has already set the story.
:::

# One Interactive View

## Toggle the strategy

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof selected = Inputs.radio(
  ["Oldest-First", "Newest-First"],
  {label: "Prioritization strategy", value: "Oldest-First"}
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
{
  const rows = metrics_js.filter((d) => d.strategy === selected)
  const data = [
    ...rows.map((d) => ({month: d.month_index, days: d.mttr_days, metric: "MTTR"})),
    ...rows.map((d) => ({month: d.month_index, days: d.mova_days, metric: "MOVA"})),
  ]

  return Plot.plot({
    width: 760,
    height: 300,
    marginTop: 20,
    color: {
      legend: true,
      domain: ["MTTR", "MOVA"],
      range: ["#355C7D", "#C05621"],
    },
    x: {label: "Month", ticks: [1, 6, 12, 18, 24]},
    y: {label: "Days", grid: true},
    style: {
      background: "#fffdfa",
      color: "#182126",
      fontFamily: "Avenir Next, Segoe UI, sans-serif",
      fontSize: "13px",
    },
    marks: [
      Plot.lineY(data, {x: "month", y: "days", stroke: "metric", strokeWidth: 3}),
      Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#d8d2c7"}),
    ],
  })
}
```

::: notes
Keep this short. Flip once. The interaction exists to reinforce the model, not to become the talk.
:::

# How To Use This

## Report a small set of metrics

| Signal | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| MTTR | closure speed |
| MOVA | age of current exposure |
| Open count | backlog size |
| 180+ days | risk stuck in the tail |

---

## Interpret them together

MTTR rising + MOVA falling = backlog cleanup

MTTR falling + MOVA rising = optics improving while exposure worsens

---

## Use metrics as diagnostics

Not as verdicts.

Not as performance theater.

# Close

## Key takeaway {.center}

::: {.metric-big .centered}
MTTR alone can mislead.

MOVA shows whether
exposure is actually shrinking.
:::

---

## Final line {.center}

If your metrics punish
the right behavior,

teams will optimize
the wrong one.

::: notes
Pause after the final line and end there.
:::
